title: Scarcity

content.statement: Items become more attractive when they appear rare or limited.
content.question: Am I using genuine scarcity my customers can verify, or manufactured urgency that will erode trust?
content.equation: Scarcity Pull = (Genuine Limit + Visibility + Time Pressure) ÷ 3
content.mental_model: Last seat at the table, desire spikes near depletion.
content.business_lens: Use real scarcity (cohort sizes, deadlines, limited inventory) transparently; avoid manufactured fake scarcity.
content.life_lens: Recognize when scarcity is being used on you; ask whether it is real or staged.
sop: Never invent fake scarcity, it costs more than it gains
content.explainer: Cialdini shows scarcity is a powerful motivator but only when authentic. Webinars with "8 spots left" that are actually unlimited get caught and damage brand. Honest cohort caps, real deadlines, and limited founding-member spots work; fake countdowns do not.
questions: Am I using genuine scarcity my customers can verify, or manufactured urgency that will erode trust?
The last deadline or 'limited spots' message you sent, could a customer have checked it and found it true?
What real constraint, capacity, inventory, your own time, could you be honest about instead of inventing a fake one?
content.equation_example: Rate each 1-10. Genuine Limit 9 (how real the constraint is), Visibility 6, Time Pressure 5. Score = (9 + 6 + 5) ÷ 3 = 6.7. Higher means urgency drives action without damaging trust because it's real; lower means the scarcity is either invisible or not credible enough to move anyone.
content.closing_statement: Urgency only works as long as it's true.

caption:
Principle Nº 304: Scarcity

Items become more attractive when they appear rare or limited. Cialdini shows scarcity is a powerful motivator but only when authentic.

Ask yourself: Am I using genuine scarcity my customers can verify, or manufactured urgency that will erode trust?
